data.name: Classic Indian Rice Pudding
data.description: A rich, velvety traditional dessert featuring basmati rice slowly simmered in full-fat milk, infused with aromatic cardamom and saffron, and crowned with toasted pistachios.

refrigerator.notes: Store in an airtight container. The pudding will thicken significantly as it chills; stir in a splash of milk when reheating or serving cold.

freezer.notes: Freezing alters the texture of the rice and causes the dairy to separate. Not recommended.

storage.reheating: Gently warm on the stovetop over low heat, adding a splash of milk to loosen the consistency.

items.name: whole milk
items.notes: Full-fat milk is essential for texture

items.name: granulated sugar
items.notes: Adjust up to 75g for a sweeter pudding

instructions.text: Place the thoroughly rinsed basmati rice into a mixing bowl. Cover with cold water and allow to soak for 30 minutes. Once soaked, drain the rice completely and set aside.

doneness_cues.visual: Rice grains appear slightly opaque and swollen

instructions.text: Pour the whole milk into a heavy-bottomed pot and place over medium-high heat. Bring to a gentle boil, reaching approximately 93 C / 200 F. Stir occasionally to prevent a skin from forming on the surface.

doneness_cues.visual: Small, active bubbles forming around the edges of the pot

instructions.text: Reduce the heat to low and carefully add the drained rice to the boiling milk. Simmer gently, stirring frequently with a wooden spoon. Be sure to scrape the bottom and sides of the pot to prevent scorching. Continue cooking for 35 to 40 minutes.

doneness_cues.visual: Milk has reduced by nearly half and has taken on an ivory hue; rice grains are plump, split, and suspended in the liquid
doneness_cues.tactile: Rice mashes completely smooth between two fingers

tips: Do not leave the pot unattended for long periods; the sugars in milk can easily burn.
Stir in a figure-eight motion to ensure the entire bottom surface is scraped.

instructions.text: Add the granulated sugar, green cardamom powder, and saffron threads to the pot. Mix well to combine. Cook for an additional 5 minutes to allow the sugar to completely dissolve and the flavors to meld.

doneness_cues.visual: Pudding is thick, glossy, and leaves a clear track when the wooden spoon is drawn through it
doneness_cues.tactile: Sugar granules can no longer be felt on the bottom of the pot

instructions.text: Remove the pot from the heat. Stir in the rose water. Ladle the hot pudding into serving bowls and top generously with the chopped pistachios and slivered almonds. Serve immediately while warm, or allow to cool and chill before serving.

troubleshooting.symptom: Milk scorches and sticks to the bottom of the pot
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Heat was too high, pot bottom was too thin, or stirring was too infrequent
troubleshooting.prevention: Always use a thick, heavy-bottomed pot, maintain a gentle simmer, and continuously scrape the bottom while cooking
troubleshooting.fix: Do not scrape the burnt layer. Immediately pour the unburnt portion of the pudding into a fresh pot and continue cooking. If the mixture already tastes burnt, it cannot be fixed.

troubleshooting.symptom: Rice remains hard even after 40 minutes of cooking
troubleshooting.likely_cause: Rice was not soaked prior to cooking, or the simmer was too low
troubleshooting.prevention: Ensure basmati rice is soaked for at least 30 minutes to jumpstart the hydration process
troubleshooting.fix: Add an extra splash of milk, cover the pot, and simmer for an additional 10 to 15 minutes

chef_notes: Soaking the basmati rice is a non-negotiable step; it allows the grains to cook evenly, preventing chalky centers, and releases starches that naturally thicken the milk.
Using full-fat whole milk is strictly recommended. Lower fat milks will yield a watery consistency and lack the luxurious mouthfeel characteristic of authentic kheer.
For an extra layer of nutty depth, lightly toast the chopped pistachios and almonds in half a teaspoon of ghee before using them as a garnish.

The pudding will continue to thicken significantly as it cools due to the starches in the rice. If it becomes too stiff in the refrigerator, simply fold in cold milk a tablespoon at a time until the desired consistency is restored.
data.cultural_context: Kheer is one of the most ancient and revered desserts in the Indian subcontinent, with roots tracing back to early Ayurvedic texts where it was considered a restorative food. It is an essential component of festive celebrations like Diwali, weddings, and religious ceremonies, symbolizing purity, joy, and prosperity. While regional variations exist throughout India, such as Payasam in the South, this slow-simmered basmati and milk preparation remains the quintessential classic.
